title: Implement API authentication, input validation, and rate limiting for PM server endpoints
description: Harden the cto-pm Elysia server by adding authentication, input validation, and rate limiting to all /api/* endpoints. This includes requiring Bearer token or JWT authentication, validating and sanitizing all input (especially for the pipeline intake endpoint), and implementing application-level rate limiting. Additionally, ensure JWT signature verification is performed in both the Next.js middleware and the PM server, and implement a secure refresh token mechanism. Never store user credentials on the client for silent re-authentication.
subtasks: Add authentication middleware to all /api/* routes in the Elysia server, requiring a Bearer token or JWT validated against a shared secret or public key.
Implement the /api/auth/login endpoint in the PM server to issue JWTs for authenticated users.
In the Next.js dashboard, update middleware to fully verify JWT signatures using the server's public key or shared secret, not just decode/expiry check.
Implement a secure refresh token mechanism with rotation on use; never store user credentials on the client.
Define a PRD schema using Zod or TypeBox and validate all POST /api/pipeline/intake payloads against this schema.
Sanitize all user-provided markdown content to prevent HTML injection in Linear/GitHub/Discord outputs.
Add application-level rate limiting to the Elysia server using Elysia's rate-limit plugin or custom middleware, with stricter limits on POST /api/pipeline/intake (e.g., 5 req/min).
Update API error handling to return 401 for unauthenticated requests, 429 for rate-limited requests, and 400 for invalid input.
Run OWASP ZAP scan against all API endpoints to verify authentication, input validation, and rate limiting are enforced.
acceptance_criteria: All /api/* endpoints require a valid Bearer token or JWT; unauthenticated requests receive 401.
JWTs are fully verified (signature, expiry, issuer) in both Next.js middleware and PM server.
Refresh tokens are implemented securely and rotated on use; no user credentials are stored on the client.
POST /api/pipeline/intake rejects invalid or malicious payloads with 400; only valid PRD schema is accepted.
Markdown content is sanitized to prevent HTML/script injection in all downstream systems.
Rate limiting is enforced at the application level (e.g., 5 req/min for intake, 60 req/min for status endpoints); 429 returned on excess.
OWASP ZAP scan reports no unauthenticated access, input validation, or rate limiting vulnerabilities.

title: Implement RBAC and ServiceAccount scoping for sigma-1 core pipeline
description: Move ServiceAccount creation and basic Role/RoleBinding for cto-pm pods from stretch tasks into Task 1 scope. Create a dedicated ServiceAccount (sigma-1-pipeline-sa) with least-privilege Role and RoleBinding, granting only read access to ConfigMaps and Secrets in the sigma-1 namespace. Assign this ServiceAccount to all cto-pm pods and ensure no pod runs with the default ServiceAccount. The secret-validation-job should use its own scoped ServiceAccount.
subtasks: Create a ServiceAccount named sigma-1-pipeline-sa in the sigma-1 namespace.
Create a Role granting get/list/watch on ConfigMaps and Secrets (no write) in sigma-1.
Create a RoleBinding linking sigma-1-pipeline-sa to the Role.
Update cto-pm Deployment to use sigma-1-pipeline-sa.
Update secret-validation-job to use a dedicated ServiceAccount with only get/list on Secrets.
Verify no pod in sigma-1 runs with the default ServiceAccount.
Test RBAC by attempting forbidden actions (e.g., create Secret) from cto-pm pod and confirming access is denied.
acceptance_criteria: All cto-pm pods run as sigma-1-pipeline-sa, not the default ServiceAccount.
sigma-1-pipeline-sa has only get/list/watch on ConfigMaps and Secrets in sigma-1; no write or cross-namespace access.
Secret-validation-job uses a ServiceAccount with only the minimum required permissions.
Attempting to create or modify Secrets from cto-pm pod fails with RBAC denied.
Pipeline and secret-validation-job both complete successfully with new RBAC in place.

title: Harden GitHub and Linear token usage: scope, separation, and documentation
description: Restrict the scope and usage of GitHub and Linear API tokens. Document and enforce the use of fine-grained GitHub PATs or GitHub App tokens scoped only to the 5dlabs/sigma-1 repository, and separate tokens for CI/test and pipeline operations. For Linear, create a separate API token for E2E testing, scoped to a test team/project, and store it as a distinct ExternalSecret. Update E2E test adapters to use the test token and ensure test cleanup logic cannot affect production resources. Implement safety checks in E2E cleanup to prevent accidental deletion or closure of production issues/PRs.
subtasks: Document the minimum required GitHub token scopes (contents:write, pull_requests:write) and ensure tokens are scoped to 5dlabs/sigma-1 only.
Switch from classic PATs to fine-grained PATs or GitHub App installation tokens where possible.
Create a separate GitHub token for CI/test operations and update E2E test adapters to use it.
Create a separate Linear API token for E2E testing, scoped to a test team/project, and store as sigma-1-linear-test-token ExternalSecret.
Update E2E test code to use the test Linear token and test GitHub token, not the production tokens.
Implement a safety check in E2E cleanup logic that refuses to archive/close issues or PRs that do not match the test run's unique identifiers (e.g., test branch prefix, test issue prefix).
Update documentation to reflect token separation and scoping requirements.
Test by running E2E tests and verifying that only test resources are affected, and that production resources are untouched even if cleanup logic is faulty.
acceptance_criteria: GitHub tokens used by the pipeline and E2E tests are fine-grained and scoped only to 5dlabs/sigma-1.
Separate tokens are used for production and test operations for both GitHub and Linear.
E2E test adapters use the test tokens and cannot affect production issues or PRs.
E2E cleanup logic refuses to delete/close resources that do not match the test run's unique identifiers.
Documentation clearly describes token scopes, separation, and rotation procedures.
Test runs confirm that only test resources are created/cleaned up, and production resources remain unaffected.

title: Apply default-deny NetworkPolicy and explicit allow rules for sigma-1 namespace
description: Apply a basic default-deny ingress NetworkPolicy in the sigma-1 namespace from the start of the project. Add explicit allow rules for known traffic patterns (e.g., ingress from Ingress controller to cto-pm, egress to required in-cluster and external services). Document intended egress restrictions and leave egress open initially if needed. This reduces the attack surface during all development and validation phases.
subtasks: Create a NetworkPolicy in sigma-1 that denies all ingress by default.
Add allow rules for ingress traffic from the Ingress controller to cto-pm on the required port.
Document intended egress restrictions and which services will require future egress allow rules.
Test that pods in sigma-1 cannot be reached from other namespaces except as explicitly allowed.
Test that cto-pm remains reachable via the intended ingress path after policy is applied.
acceptance_criteria: Default-deny NetworkPolicy is applied in sigma-1 namespace.
Only explicitly allowed ingress traffic (e.g., from Ingress controller) can reach cto-pm.
Pods in sigma-1 are not reachable from other namespaces unless allowed.
Pipeline and services remain functional after policy is applied.
Documentation exists for intended egress restrictions and future lockdown steps.

title: Pin container images to digest and enable image scanning in core pipeline
description: Update all Kubernetes manifests to reference container images by SHA256 digest instead of mutable tags. Document approved base images and their digests. Move Trivy (or equivalent) image scanning to run before E2E tests (Task 8) as a pre-deployment gate, rather than as a post-deployment stretch task.
subtasks: Update all container image references (bitnami/kubectl, curlimages/curl, aquasec/trivy, etc.) in Kubernetes manifests to use SHA256 digests.
Document the approved base images and their digests in a shared README or manifest.
Integrate Trivy (or equivalent) image scanning as a CI step or CronJob that runs before E2E tests.
Fail the pipeline if HIGH/CRITICAL vulnerabilities are found in any image.
Test by running Trivy scan on all images and verifying that only approved, scanned images are deployed.
acceptance_criteria: All container images in manifests are referenced by SHA256 digest.
Documentation lists all approved images and their digests.
Trivy (or equivalent) scan runs before E2E tests and blocks on HIGH/CRITICAL findings.
No unscanned or mutable-tag images are deployed.
CI logs show image scan results and pass/fail status.

title: Enforce TLS verification for all external API calls with secrets
description: Ensure that all external API calls (e.g., NOUS_API_KEY to external API) are made with TLS certificate verification enabled. Add a startup check in the Bun/Elysia server that NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED is not set to 0. Document that all external API calls must use verified TLS connections.
subtasks: Audit all external API calls in the codebase to ensure they use https:// URLs.
Add a startup assertion in the Bun/Elysia server that NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED is not 0.
Document in the developer README that all external API calls must use verified TLS.
Test by attempting to set NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 and verifying the server refuses to start.
Run a network sniffer or log to confirm that all secret-bearing API calls use HTTPS with valid certificates.
acceptance_criteria: All external API calls with secrets use HTTPS and verify TLS certificates.
Server refuses to start if NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0.
Documentation clearly states TLS verification is mandatory.
No secrets are sent over unverified or plaintext connections.
